evidence: Production logic is clean of AI hallmarks: no entry/exit debug traces, no restating inline comments (e.g. '// increment i' over i++), no generic names like handleData or processItem in domain code — everything uses corpus-domain terms: executeCollocations, buildAndRankCollocates, prefetchCorpusFrequencies, findCollocateIntersection.
Error handling is centralized in HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling — not copied verbatim across files. No boilerplate repeated across handler classes.

QueryExecutor interface still carries a '

Method responsibilities

' Ul block at class level that cross-references each method, largely duplicating what the individual method Javadocs already say (e.g. executeCqlQuery is described twice: in the class block and in its own Javadoc). This accumulation pattern is characteristic of AI-generated documentation layering.

CollocateQueryHelper.forTesting() factory: its 2-sentence Javadoc explains null-index semantics and NullPointerException risk on a 1-line implementation. The Javadoc serves a real protective purpose (warning callers), but remains longer than the method body it documents.

Stale RelationPatternUtils cross-references appear in two Javadocs: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern param doc ('typically by the caller via RelationPatternUtils#buildFullPattern') and RelationConfig class doc ('Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in RelationPatternUtils'). No such class exists in the codebase — the class was likely renamed or merged into RelationUtils during refactoring, leaving AI-generated docs pointing to a ghost.

executeCqlQuery @implNote says 'Returns token windows matching the pattern without ranked collocation data' — the trailing clause restates what 'raw concordance results' in the method summary already communicates.
registerPostHandler has a one-sentence Javadoc 'Mirrors registerGetHandler for POST routes' on a 1-line pass-through body. Minor docstring-on-trivial-method bloat.

warnIfWildcardCors Javadoc was previously flagged as multi-paragraph; the current version has a single

tag and an @throws — concise and informative. This open issue appears resolved.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely clean of AI-generated debt at the logic level: naming is domain-specific throughout, error handling is centralized rather than copy-pasted, and inline comments explain constraints and non-obvious decisions rather than restating code. The remaining AI debt is almost entirely concentrated in Javadoc quality: cross-referencing prose that duplicates individual method descriptions, a stale reference to a non-existent RelationPatternUtils class across two files, and occasional @implNote bloat that restates the method summary.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A score of 72 reflects a codebase with no systemic AI debt in production logic, but with identifiable Javadoc-layer accumulation (the QueryExecutor 'Method responsibilities' block, stale RelationPatternUtils references, minor docstring-on-trivial-method cases) that a developer must mentally filter while navigating key interfaces. The open forTesting() and QueryExecutor issues are real but isolated; they do not indicate a pattern of AI-generated logic or broad documentation overreach, placing the score solidly in the 70–75 range rather than below 65.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor class-level 'Method responsibilities' block duplicates individual method Javadocs
evidence:
The interface Javadoc contains a '

Method responsibilities

'
    that describes all four methods, including distinguishing notes ('Distinct from executeCqlQuery') that are already present in the respective method Javadocs.
executeCqlQuery is described at class level ('Returns raw KWIC results without ranking') and again in its own Javadoc and @implNote, so a developer reads the same information in three places.
issues.suggestion: Remove the 'Method responsibilities' <ul> from the class-level Javadoc. The interface-level comment should explain the design rationale (why two ports exist, who should declare which) without restating per-method behaviour that the method Javadocs cover. The cross-method distinction notes belong inside the individual method Javadocs where they already appear.

issues.summary: Two Javadocs reference non-existent RelationPatternUtils class after apparent rename/merge
evidence: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern @param bcqlPattern: 'typically by the caller via RelationPatternUtils#buildFullPattern' — RelationPatternUtils does not exist; the utility lives in RelationUtils.
RelationConfig class Javadoc: 'Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in RelationPatternUtils' — same ghost reference. The logic actually lives in RelationUtils.
issues.suggestion: Replace both 'RelationPatternUtils' references with '{@link RelationUtils}'. In RelationConfig, update the Javadoc to reference the actual class. In QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern, update the @param cross-reference or remove the 'typically by the caller via ...' clause if it no longer accurately describes the usage.

root_causes: Javadoc written (or drafted by AI) ahead of or alongside refactoring: when RelationPatternUtils was merged into RelationUtils, the prose docs were not updated, leaving stale cross-references.
Additive documentation style: the QueryExecutor class-level Javadoc accumulated a summary section as methods were added, without pruning it as each method gained its own thorough Javadoc.

add.header: Production logic is free of AI hallmarks
add.description: All domain identifiers use corpus-specific terms (executeCollocations, buildAndRankCollocates, findCollocateIntersection). Inline comments explain constraints and non-obvious decisions, not code mechanics. Error handling is centralized, not copy-pasted. These are strong positive signals that logic-level AI debt is absent.

add.header: Stale RelationPatternUtils ghost references in two Javadocs
add.description: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern and RelationConfig both reference a RelationPatternUtils class that does not exist. The logic was merged into RelationUtils during refactoring but the Javadocs were not updated. This is a localised documentation-rot artifact, not a logic debt.

The previously flagged 'multi-paragraph restating Javadoc' on warnIfWildcardCors has been trimmed to a single-paragraph opt-in explanation plus @throws. The current version is concise and non-restating.
